Course Catalog Entry
Course ID
248287

Clean Enrollware Title
American Red Cross BLS Provider – Blended Learning

Landing Page Title
Red Cross BLS Certification – Online Course with Skills Check

URL Slug
/red-cross-bls-online

Lander Page Course Copy
<h2>Red Cross BLS Certification – Online Course with Skills Check</h2>

<p>
The American Red Cross Basic Life Support (BLS) blended learning course gives students a flexible way to complete BLS certification by combining online coursework with an in-person skills check.
</p>

<p>
This format is ideal for healthcare professionals, students, and staff who need Red Cross BLS certification but prefer to complete the knowledge portion on their own schedule before attending a hands-on evaluation.
</p>

<h3>How the Course Works</h3>

<ul>
<li>Complete the American Red Cross BLS online course and test</li>
<li>Schedule and attend an in-person ARC BLS skills check</li>
<li>Present your online completion certificate at the session</li>
<li>Receive your Red Cross BLS digital certificate after successful completion</li>
</ul>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Healthcare professionals who need ARC BLS certification</li>
<li>Medical and dental office staff</li>
<li>Home healthcare workers</li>
<li>Clinical and allied health students</li>
<li>Anyone whose employer or school specifically requires Red Cross BLS</li>
</ul>

<h3>Why Choose Blended Learning</h3>

<p>
Blended learning allows students to complete the coursework at their own pace and then finish with a focused, instructor-led skills session.
</p>

<p>
This can be a convenient option for students with busy schedules who still need a recognized, hands-on BLS certification pathway.
</p>

<h3>Course Format</h3>

<p>
This course combines:
</p>

<ul>
<li><strong>Online learning</strong> for the cognitive portion</li>
<li><strong>In-person skills testing</strong> with an ARC-certified instructor</li>
</ul>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
Upon successful completion, students receive a
<strong>2-year American Red Cross BLS digital certificate</strong>.
</p>

<p>
Certification records remain accessible online through the Red Cross system.
</p>

<h3>Why Students Choose 910CPR</h3>

<ul>
<li>Flexible scheduling</li>
<li>Instructor-led skills validation</li>
<li>Practical hands-on evaluation</li>
<li>Reliable class availability</li>
<li>Recognized Red Cross certification</li>
</ul>
SEO Description
Complete Red Cross BLS certification with flexible online learning and an in-person skills check. Ideal for healthcare professionals, students, and staff who need ARC BLS certification with a 2-year digital certificate.

An SEO-friendly description of the course. Recommended length: 240 characters. Maximum length: 500 characters
AHA Heartsaver® Pediatric First Aid CPR AED (in-person). Instructor-led training for childcare providers—daycare/preschool staff, teachers, camp counselors, coaches, foster parents—for work or regulatory compliance. Learn pediatric first aid (bleeding, injuries, allergic reactions, asthma, poisoning), medical & environmental emergencies, plus infant/child CPR and AED. Includes skills check and AHA eCard valid 2 years. Optional adult CPR/AED available. Pediatric first aid CPR AED class near me.

<div style="text-align: center; background-color: #f47b20; color: white; padding: 10px; font-weight: bold;">AHA - Heartsaver&reg; Pediatric First Aid / CPR / AED &ndash; In-person</div>
<p style="text-align: justify;"><img style="width: 25px; float: left; margin-right: 5px;" src="https://www.enrollware.com/sitefiles/coastalcprtraining/_AHA.png" alt="" />The <strong>American Heart Association (AHA) Heartsaver&reg; Pediatric First Aid CPR AED</strong> course is designed for anyone involved in childcare who needs a certification for work or regulatory purposes, including daycare staff, camp counselors, teachers, coaches, foster parents, and others responsible for children&rsquo;s safety.</p>
<p style="text-align: justify;">This <strong>instructor-led, classroom-based training</strong> provides participants with the knowledge and hands-on skills to respond to pediatric medical emergencies, injuries, and cardiac arrest. The course follows the latest AHA guidelines and includes both first aid and CPR/AED instruction focused on infants and children.</p>
<h3>🩹 Course Topics Include:</h3>
<ul>
<li>First aid basics and injury emergencies (bleeding, sprains, broken bones, bites, and stings)</li>
<li>Medical emergencies (asthma, allergic reactions, diabetes, seizures)</li>
<li>Environmental emergencies (heat/cold-related issues, poisoning)</li>
<li>Child and infant CPR and AED use</li>
<li>Optional: Adult CPR and AED (if needed for job requirements)</li>
</ul>
<h3>📘 Required Materials:</h3>
<p style="text-align: justify;">All participants must have access to a current <strong>AHA Heartsaver&reg; Pediatric First Aid CPR AED Student Workbook</strong>, available in either:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Printed manual:</strong> Offered during registration</li>
<li><strong>Digital eBook:</strong> Emailed upon registration</li>
</ul>
<h3>📜 Certification:</h3>
<p style="text-align: justify;">Successful completion includes skills testing by an AHA Instructor and issuance of a <strong>2-year AHA Heartsaver&reg; Pediatric First Aid CPR AED eCard</strong>, valid nationwide.</p>

Course Catalog Entry
Course ID
351632

Clean Enrollware Title
AHA Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid CPR AED – In-Person

Landing Page Title
Childcare CPR & First Aid Certification (AHA Pediatric CPR AED)

URL Slug
/pediatric-first-aid-cpr-aed

Lander Page Copy
<h2>Childcare CPR & First Aid Certification (AHA Pediatric CPR AED)</h2>

<p>
The American Heart Association (AHA) Heartsaver® Pediatric First Aid CPR AED course is designed for people responsible for the safety of infants and children. This instructor-led class teaches participants how to recognize and respond to pediatric medical emergencies, injuries, and cardiac arrest.
</p>

<p>
This course is commonly required for childcare workers, daycare staff, teachers, coaches, camp counselors, and foster parents who must maintain CPR and First Aid certification for employment or regulatory compliance.
</p>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Daycare and preschool staff</li>
<li>Childcare providers and babysitters</li>
<li>Teachers and school staff</li>
<li>Camp counselors and youth program leaders</li>
<li>Foster parents and guardians</li>
<li>Coaches and youth sports staff</li>
</ul>

<h3>What You Will Learn</h3>

<ul>
<li>First aid basics and scene safety</li>
<li>Treatment of injuries such as bleeding, burns, fractures, bites, and stings</li>
<li>Medical emergencies including allergic reactions, asthma, diabetes, and seizures</li>
<li>Environmental emergencies such as heat illness, hypothermia, and poisoning</li>
<li>Infant and child CPR</li>
<li>AED use for infants and children</li>
<li>Choking response for infants and children</li>
</ul>

<h3>Childcare Compliance</h3>

<p>
This course fulfills the CPR and First Aid training requirement for many childcare and youth program positions. It is widely accepted for:
</p>

<ul>
<li>North Carolina childcare providers (NC DCDEE CPR/First Aid requirement)</li>
<li>Daycare and preschool licensing programs</li>
<li>Youth sports organizations</li>
<li>Private childcare employment requirements</li>
</ul>

<p>
Additional state-specific childcare training may still be required depending on employer or licensing authority.
</p>

<h3>Course Format</h3>

<ul>
<li>Instructor-led classroom training</li>
<li>Hands-on CPR and first aid skills practice</li>
<li>Skills testing with an AHA instructor</li>
</ul>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
Students who successfully complete the course receive a 
<strong>2-year American Heart Association Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid CPR AED eCard</strong>, valid nationwide.
</p>

<p>
This certification demonstrates that the participant has completed pediatric CPR and first aid training according to the latest American Heart Association guidelines.
</p>
SEO Description
Childcare CPR & First Aid certification with AHA Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid CPR AED training. Ideal for daycare staff, teachers, coaches, and childcare providers. Includes hands-on training and a 2-year AHA certification card.

<!-- Header Section -->
<div style="text-align: left; background-color: #b30000; color: white; padding: 10px; font-weight: bold;">🚨 BLS Challenge &ndash; Assessment-Only Option for Experienced Providers</div>
<!-- Course Overview -->
<p style="text-align: justify; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">&nbsp;</p>
<p style="text-align: justify; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">This is an <strong>assessment-only session</strong>&mdash;an American Red Cross BLS Challenge&mdash;designed for experienced providers who need to <strong>renew their certification</strong>. There is <strong>no instruction or remediation</strong> during this session.</p>
<p style="text-align: justify; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">&nbsp;</p>
<!-- Important Notes -->
<p style="text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;"><strong>🔥 IMPORTANT:</strong></p>
<ul style="text-indent: 0px !important; margin-left: 15px !important;">
<li>You must <span style="text-decoration: underline;">demonstrate all BLS skills</span> and <span style="text-decoration: underline;">pass a written test</span> to earn certification.</li>
<li>This is <strong>not a training course</strong>. You must already know the skills <strong>like the back of your hand</strong>.</li>
<li><strong>If you fail the Challenge</strong>, you will not be certified and must complete the full BLS course at a later date.</li>
</ul>
<!-- Preparation Note -->
<p style="text-align: justify; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">💡 We <strong>strongly recommend</strong> that you purchase the ARC BLS Provider Manual (or use reliable online resources) to review and practice before attempting the Challenge. There are also <strong>non-endorsed YouTube videos</strong> that can help&mdash;search for "ARC BLS skills demonstration" for reference.</p>
<p style="text-align: justify; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">&nbsp;</p>
<!-- Refund Policy -->
<p style="text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;"><strong>⚠️ No refunds for Challenge failures.</strong></p>
<p style="text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">&nbsp;</p>
<!-- Confidence Statement -->
<p style="text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0px !important; text-align: center;"><span style="font-size: 14pt;"><strong>Confidence is key.</strong> Know it, show it, and get certified!</span></p>

Good — this is a specialty course type and worth keeping because it targets confident renewals + experienced providers. I’ve stored the structured version below.

Course Catalog Entry
Course ID
444919

Clean Enrollware Title
ARC BLS Challenge – Skills Assessment Renewal

Landing Page Title
Red Cross BLS Challenge (Assessment-Only Renewal)

URL Slug
/red-cross-bls-challenge

Lander Page Copy
<h2>Red Cross BLS Challenge – Assessment-Only Renewal</h2>

<p>
The American Red Cross BLS Challenge is an <strong>assessment-only option</strong> designed for experienced healthcare providers who already know the BLS skills and simply need to renew their certification.
</p>

<p>
This session is not a traditional class. There is <strong>no instruction or remediation</strong>. Participants must demonstrate the required skills and pass the written assessment to earn certification.
</p>

<h3>Who This Option Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Experienced providers who already know Red Cross BLS skills</li>
<li>Healthcare professionals who recently completed BLS training</li>
<li>Providers confident in their ability to pass skills testing without instruction</li>
</ul>

<h3>Important Requirements</h3>

<ul>
<li>You must successfully demonstrate all required BLS skills</li>
<li>You must pass the Red Cross BLS written exam</li>
<li>This is <strong>not a training class</strong></li>
<li>No coaching or remediation is provided during the challenge</li>
</ul>

<h3>If You Do Not Pass</h3>

<p>
Participants who do not successfully complete the challenge will need to register for the full Red Cross BLS course at a later date.
</p>

<p>
Because this is an assessment session, <strong>challenge attempts are non-refundable</strong>.
</p>

<h3>Preparation Tips</h3>

<p>
We strongly recommend reviewing the Red Cross BLS Provider Manual or other reputable study materials before attempting the challenge.
</p>

<p>
You should be comfortable performing all required BLS skills for adult, child, and infant patients prior to attending the assessment.
</p>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
Participants who successfully complete the skills assessment and written test receive a
<strong>2-year American Red Cross BLS digital certificate</strong>.
</p>

<p>
Certification records remain available online through the Red Cross training system.
</p>
SEO Description
Fast Red Cross BLS renewal for experienced providers. The ARC BLS Challenge is an assessment-only option with skills testing and written exam for confident healthcare professionals needing BLS recertification.

Course Catalog Entry
Course ID
359827

Clean Enrollware Title
USCG Elementary First Aid & CPR – Online + Skills Session

Landing Page Title
USCG Elementary First Aid & CPR Certification (AHA + AMERHA-216)

URL Slug
/uscg-elementary-first-aid-cpr

Lander Page Copy
<h2>USCG Elementary First Aid & CPR Certification</h2>

<p>
This course provides the training required to meet the 
<strong>U.S. Coast Guard Elementary First Aid and CPR requirement</strong> 
for Merchant Mariner Credential (MMC) applicants and renewals.
</p>

<p>
The course uses the <strong>American Heart Association Heartsaver® First Aid CPR AED</strong> program combined with documentation required for U.S. Coast Guard compliance.
</p>

<h3>How the Course Works</h3>

<ul>
<li>Complete the online Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED coursework</li>
<li>Attend a short in-person skills session with an instructor</li>
<li>Demonstrate CPR and first aid skills</li>
<li>Receive certification the same day</li>
</ul>

<h3>Certification Issued</h3>

<ul>
<li><strong>AHA Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED eCard</strong> (valid for 2 years)</li>
<li><strong>AMERHA-216 documentation</strong> accepted by the U.S. Coast Guard</li>
</ul>

<p>
Both documents are typically issued the same day after successful completion of the skills session.
</p>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Merchant Mariner Credential (MMC) applicants</li>
<li>Boat captains and maritime professionals</li>
<li>Commercial vessel crew members</li>
<li>Maritime workers needing USCG-accepted first aid certification</li>
</ul>

<h3>USCG Regulatory References</h3>

<p>
This training meets the Elementary First Aid requirements referenced by:
</p>

<ul>
<li>STCW Code Table A-VI/1-3 – Elementary First Aid</li>
<li>46 CFR 11.302(a)(3) and 12.602(a)(3)</li>
<li>46 CFR 11.201(i)(1) – First Aid and CPR training for mariner credentialing</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Content</h3>

<ul>
<li>Adult CPR and AED use</li>
<li>First aid assessment and emergency response</li>
<li>Treatment for bleeding, burns, and injuries</li>
<li>Medical emergencies such as allergic reactions and cardiac arrest</li>
<li>Choking response</li>
</ul>

<h3>Flexible Training Format</h3>

<p>
The blended format allows you to complete most of the coursework online at your own pace before attending a brief hands-on skills session.
</p>

<p>
You receive the <strong>same AHA certification card</strong> as the traditional classroom course.
</p>
SEO Description
USCG Elementary First Aid and CPR certification for Merchant Mariner Credential (MMC). Includes AHA Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED training and AMERHA-216 documentation accepted by the U.S. Coast Guard.

<h2>USCG Captain License First Aid & CPR Certification</h2>

<p>
This course provides the training required to meet the 
<strong>U.S. Coast Guard Elementary First Aid and CPR requirement</strong> 
for Merchant Mariner Credential (MMC) applicants and Captain License holders.
</p>

<p>
The training is based on the 
<strong>American Heart Association Heartsaver® First Aid CPR AED program</strong> 
and includes the documentation required for U.S. Coast Guard compliance.
</p>

<h3>Convenient Blended Learning Format</h3>

<p>
Students complete the course in two simple steps:
</p>

<ul>
<li>Complete the online Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED coursework</li>
<li>Attend a brief in-person skills session with an instructor</li>
</ul>

<p>
After completing the skills session, students receive their certification documents the same day.
</p>

<h3>Certification Issued</h3>

<ul>
<li><strong>AHA Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED eCard</strong> (valid for 2 years)</li>
<li><strong>AMERHA-216 documentation</strong> accepted by the U.S. Coast Guard</li>
</ul>

<h3>USCG Regulatory References</h3>

<ul>
<li>STCW Code Table A-VI/1-3 – Elementary First Aid</li>
<li>46 CFR 11.302(a)(3) and 12.602(a)(3)</li>
<li>46 CFR 11.201(i)(1) – First Aid and CPR for mariner credentialing</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Topics</h3>

<ul>
<li>Adult CPR and AED use</li>
<li>First aid assessment and scene safety</li>
<li>Treatment of injuries and bleeding</li>
<li>Medical emergencies such as allergic reactions and cardiac arrest</li>
<li>Choking response</li>
</ul>

<p>
This training results in the <strong>same AHA certification card issued in traditional classroom courses</strong>, but allows students to complete the learning portion online at their own pace.
</p>

<p style="text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important; padding: 0 !important;"><img style="float: right !important; padding: 7px !important; margin: 0 0 10px 10px !important;" src="https://www.enrollware.com/sitefiles/coastalcprtraining/Class_Images/HSI_BLS_FA_BL.jpg" alt="HSI Blended Class" width="210" /></p>
<!-- Course Overview -->
<p style="text-align: justify; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">This HSI blended learning course combines <strong>Basic Life Support (BLS)</strong> and <strong>Adult First Aid/CPR/AED</strong> into a single certification. It's perfect for workplaces that need both credentials to meet insurance or licensing requirements. Participants complete an online course and then attend a brief in-person skills session with an authorized HSI instructor.</p>
<p style="text-align: justify; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">&nbsp;</p>
<!-- Centered Image -->
<p style="text-align: center; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;"><img style="border: 1px solid #ccc; padding: 5px; box-shadow: 2px 2px 5px rgba(0,0,0,0.2);" src="https://otis.osmanager4.com/assets/images/otis.png" alt="HSI Blended Online Course" width="200" /></p>
<p style="text-align: center; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">&nbsp;</p>
<!-- Course Description -->
<p style="text-align: justify; text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;">Once registered, students receive a link to the online portion, which covers adult CPR, AED use, First Aid basics, and professional-level BLS topics such as team response, airway support, and high-performance compressions. After completing the online section, students attend a short in-person session to demonstrate hands-on skills and receive real-time feedback.<br /><br /></p>
<!-- Course Highlights -->
<p style="text-indent: 0px !important; margin: 0 !important;"><strong>Course Highlights:</strong></p>
<ul style="text-indent: 0px !important; margin-left: 15px !important;">

<li>Ideal for professional offices, medical/dental staff, and facilities requiring dual compliance</li>

<li>Attend a 30&ndash;60 minute in-person skills session</li>

</ul>

<h2>BLS + First Aid Certification (HSI Blended Learning)</h2>

<p>
This course combines <strong>Basic Life Support (BLS)</strong> and 
<strong>Adult First Aid / CPR / AED</strong> into a single certification program.
</p>

<p>
The blended learning format allows students to complete the training online and then attend a short in-person session to demonstrate the required hands-on skills.
</p>

<h3>How the Course Works</h3>

<ul>
<li>Receive access to the HSI online training program</li>
<li>Complete the coursework at your own pace</li>
<li>Schedule a short in-person skills session</li>
<li>Demonstrate CPR, AED, and First Aid skills</li>
<li>Receive certification after successful completion</li>
</ul>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Behavioral health providers (RBTs, BCBA staff)</li>
<li>ABA therapy teams</li>
<li>Medical or dental offices</li>
<li>Professional workplaces needing both BLS and First Aid</li>
<li>Organizations that require dual certification for compliance</li>
</ul>

<h3>Compliance and Workplace Requirements</h3>

<p>
This course is commonly used to meet requirements for:
</p>

<ul>
<li>Trillium behavioral health provider requirements</li>
<li>TRICARE BLS requirements for ABA providers</li>
<li>Insurance and licensing standards requiring BLS and First Aid</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Topics</h3>

<ul>
<li>Adult CPR and AED use</li>
<li>High-performance BLS techniques</li>
<li>First aid assessment and scene safety</li>
<li>Treatment of injuries and bleeding</li>
<li>Medical emergencies such as allergic reactions and seizures</li>
<li>Workplace emergency response procedures</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Format</h3>

<ul>
<li>Self-paced online training</li>
<li>Instructor-led in-person skills evaluation</li>
<li>Typical skills session: 30–60 minutes</li>
</ul>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
Upon successful completion, students receive a 
<strong>2-year HSI certification</strong> covering both Basic Life Support and Adult First Aid.
</p>

<p>
This combined certification allows organizations to meet workplace compliance requirements with a single training program.
</p>

<ul>
<li><strong>Current AHA BLS Provider Card</strong> &mdash; required before you're officially certified as an instructor. If you don't have one, you can add a BLS course to your registration and complete it before class day.</li>
<li><strong>Demo student for your monitoring day</strong> &mdash; Bring a guest to act as your student and they&rsquo;ll receive a free BLS card. Can&rsquo;t bring someone? Let us know &mdash; we&rsquo;ll help!</li>
</ul>

<h2>Become an American Heart Association BLS Instructor</h2>

This course prepares qualified candidates to become certified 
<strong>American Heart Association Basic Life Support (BLS) Instructors</strong>.

<ul>

</ul>

<li>AHA BLS Instructor Manual</li>

<li>Complete a monitored teaching session</li>
</ol>

<strong>American Heart Association BLS Instructor certification</strong>.
</p>

<p>This American Heart Association BLS Provider course is designed for healthcare professionals such as nurses, EMTs, paramedics, medical students, and other clinical staff who need initial BLS certification or whose certification has expired.</p>
<p>The class includes instructor-led training and hands-on practice covering adult, child, and infant CPR, AED use, and team-based resuscitation skills following current American Heart Association guidelines.</p>

<h2>BLS Certification for Healthcare Providers</h2>

<p>
The American Heart Association <strong>Basic Life Support (BLS) Provider course</strong> is designed for healthcare professionals who need CPR certification for clinical work.
</p>

<p>
This instructor-led class teaches high-quality CPR, AED use, and team-based resuscitation skills used in hospitals, clinics, and emergency medical settings.
</p>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Nurses and nursing students</li>
<li>EMTs and paramedics</li>
<li>Medical and dental professionals</li>
<li>Respiratory therapists</li>
<li>Physical therapists and medical staff</li>
<li>Healthcare students and clinical trainees</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Topics</h3>

<ul>
<li>Adult, child, and infant CPR</li>
<li>Automated External Defibrillator (AED) use</li>
<li>Team-based resuscitation and communication</li>
<li>Airway management and ventilation techniques</li>
<li>Recognition of cardiac arrest and respiratory emergencies</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Format</h3>

<ul>

<li>Skills testing using AHA guidelines</li>

</p>

<p>
This certification is accepted by hospitals, healthcare employers, and medical training programs nationwide.
</p>

Where We Are in the Process

For each course, we produce four outputs:

1️⃣ Clean Course Title
• No HTML
• Official AHA/ARC/HSI naming
• Works in Enrollware

2️⃣ HTML Course Description
• Customer facing
• Strong SEO
• Correct links

• Converts visitors into registrations
• Reinforces credibility for corporate safety managers

4️⃣ Short URL (SMS Friendly)
• Used by chatbot, texts, marketing
• Points to Enrollware schedule

So the next step is I produce the cleaned 4 outputs.

Yo think we should keep the lander project lean.... or, pepper all these bitches in also?     They would still be in enrollware so if it comes up or we need ARC or HSI stuff, we can do those classes but are there people out SHOPPING FOR HSI?   And AHA is significantly more sought for in this area than ARC... so...  does showing SOME ARC classes cause people to call for specific classes ... or should I build that huge catalog ?? 

This is a really good strategic question, and it actually affects the entire 26,000-page lander ecosystem you’re building.

Short answer first, then the reasoning.

Short answer:
Keep the lander network lean and AHA-dominant, and only lightly represent ARC and HSI.

Not zero… but not equal weight either.

Think of it like a restaurant menu:

You lead with the house specialty, but you still quietly list alternatives for the few people who want them.

The Reality of the Market (Especially in Coastal NC)
From what you’ve already observed with 910CPR, demand usually looks like this:

Certification	Typical demand
AHA BLS	Extremely high
AHA ACLS / PALS	High
AHA Heartsaver	High
ARC	Occasional
HSI	Mostly employer driven
People rarely Google things like:

“HSI CPR class Wilmington”

“American Red Cross BLS certification Wilmington”

They usually search:

CPR class near me

BLS class Wilmington NC

ACLS renewal Wilmington

And then they assume AHA unless told otherwise.

You already know this from running 910CPR.

What Happens If You Promote Everything Equally
If you put ARC, HSI, AHA all equally across the landers, two problems happen:

1️⃣ You dilute your strongest signal
Google prefers clear topical authority.

Example:

If most pages say:

AHA BLS Provider CPR Certification Wilmington NC

Google starts believing:

“This site is the authority for AHA BLS.”

But if pages say:

AHA / ARC / HSI / OSHA / etc everywhere…

Google gets a weaker signal.

2️⃣ Customers get confused
You know the conversation already.

Customer calls and says:

“Do I need Red Cross or American Heart?”

Your answer:

“Usually American Heart.”

If the site shows everything equally, it creates a decision problem.

Better strategy:

Let them assume AHA first.

The Ideal Structure for Your Lander Network
Think of your lander system like a pyramid.

Tier 1 (80-90% of pages)
AHA courses

Example landers:

• BLS Provider
• ACLS
• PALS
• Heartsaver CPR/AED
• Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED
• Heartcode BLS
• Heartcode ACLS
• Heartcode PALS

These are the traffic magnets.

Tier 2 (supporting)
ARC courses

Maybe:

• Red Cross BLS
• Red Cross CPR/AED

But far fewer pages.

Purpose:

“Available upon request.”

Tier 3 (corporate / employer)
HSI

This is where HSI shines.

Example messaging:

Many businesses choose HSI CPR training because certification cards can be issued directly to the employer's HR department.

That’s actually a great selling point you already use.

But HSI should appear mostly on:

• corporate safety pages
• group training pages

Not dominate the SEO landers.

What This Means for Your 26k Lander Network
For the class session landers, prioritize:

✔ AHA BLS
✔ AHA ACLS
✔ AHA PALS
✔ AHA Heartsaver
✔ Heartcode versions

Those will generate 95% of registrations.

ARC and HSI landers should exist, but much fewer.

The Smart Trick Most Training Centers Miss
Instead of pushing ARC and HSI courses, do this:

Add a small note on pages.

Example:

Prefer American Red Cross or HSI certification?
910CPR can provide those programs for groups and organizations.

That way:

• You capture searches
• You avoid confusing customers
• You signal capability

My Honest Recommendation for You
For the lander system:

Build AHA-heavy SEO infrastructure.

Let ARC and HSI appear strategically.

Because the real engine for 910CPR will be:

• AHA BLS
• AHA ACLS
• AHA PALS
• Heartsaver

Which you already know from your data.

One Last Thing (This is important)
Your lander system is not just selling seats.

It is selling:

• corporate contracts
• AED programs
• renewals
• training center credibility

So the messaging should quietly say:

“AHA is our primary program.”

But also:

“We can do anything you need.”

If you want, I can also show you something most CPR companies never figure out:

A single section you can add to every lander that dramatically increases corporate safety managers contacting you instead of just registering for one seat.

And it fits perfectly with the defensible / predictable / documented / renewable messaging you mentioned earlier.

Yes. Chat alone is not a good enough repository for a 26,000-page build.

If the rewritten HTML only lives buried in messages, then practically speaking:

it is hard to retrieve reliably

hard to batch with scripts

hard to version

hard to feed into the generator

and too easy to lose track of

It needs to become structured data
For this project, the rewritten material should end up in something like:

one JSON master file, or

one file per course, or

both

That is the right build path.

What the repository should look like
The safest structure is:

Option 1: one file per course
Example:

courses/
  209806.json
  359474.json
  210549.json
  241108.json
  209818.json

{
  "course_id": "209806",
  "original_course_name": "AHA - BLS Provider - In-person Initial",
  "clean_title": "AHA BLS Provider – In-Person",
  "lander_title": "BLS Certification for Healthcare Providers (AHA BLS Provider)",
  "url_slug": "/aha-bls-provider",
  "schedule_url": "https://coastalcprtraining.enrollware.com/schedule#ct209806",
  "cert_body": "AHA",
  "course_type": "BLS Provider",
  "price": 75.00,
  "original_html": "<p>...</p>",
  "lander_html": "<h2>...</h2>",
  "seo_description": "AHA BLS certification for healthcare providers...",
  "notes": {
    "category": "Healthcare CPR",
    "core_catalog": true
  }
}
Option 2: one master JSON file
Example:

course_archive.json
Containing an array of all courses.

That is easier for scripts.

Best real-world answer
For your build, I would use:

both
1. course_archive.json
For the generator to read all courses at once.

2. individual course files
For easy editing, troubleshooting, and replacing one course without touching the whole system.

That gives you:

machine-friendly

human-friendly

easy backup

easy diff/version control

Where should this json "exsist"   .. RAW?

Short answer: yes, it should exist as a RAW file in your repo. That is the correct architecture for what you're building.

But let me explain exactly where and why, so it actually helps the lander generator instead of becoming another loose file.

Where the JSON Should Live
Since your lander project already exists here:

Documents
  GitHub
    910cpr-class-landers
the archive should live inside that repo, something like:

910cpr-class-landers
│
├─ docs
│   ├─ data
│   │   └─ course_archive.json   ← MASTER COURSE DATABASE
│   │
│   ├─ images
│   ├─ classes
│   └─ index.html
│
├─ scripts
│
└─ build scripts
So the real path becomes:

/docs/data/course_archive.json
This keeps it:

• version controlled
• visible to the generator
• accessible to the website
• accessible to scripts

Why RAW Matters
GitHub Pages can serve raw JSON directly.

So your scripts can pull from:

https://raw.githubusercontent.com/YOURREPO/910cpr-class-landers/main/docs/data/course_archive.json
That means:

lander generator can read it

chatbot can read it

schedule builder can read it

other scripts can read it

One source of truth.

What This JSON Becomes
This file becomes the course database for everything.

Example:

course_archive.json
{
  "209806": {
    "title": "AHA BLS Provider – In-Person",
    "slug": "aha-bls-provider",
    "schedule": "https://coastalcprtraining.enrollware.com/schedule#ct209806",
    "seo": "...",
    "lander_html": "...",
    "cert_body": "AHA"
  }
}
Then your lander builder does:

build_landers.py
for course in courses:
    build_pages(course)
And that generates thousands of pages.

Why This Solves Your Earlier Concern
Earlier you said:

If the rewritten HTML resides in chat… how can you use it?

Exactly.

By moving it to JSON:

• it becomes machine readable
• scripts can reuse it
• nothing is trapped in chat

The Next Step (Important)
Right now the JSON I generated is v1.

Before we move forward, we should do one improvement:

Convert it to a dictionary keyed by Course ID
Instead of a list.

Example:

{
  "209806": { ... },
  "359474": { ... },
  "210549": { ... }
}
Why?

Because then scripts can instantly do:

course = courses["209806"]
Much faster and cleaner.

What I Recommend Next
Before generating more courses, we should:

Step 1
Place the file here:

/docs/data/course_archive.json
Step 2
Commit to GitHub.

Step 3
Then every new course we process gets appended to that file.

One Small But Powerful Upgrade
Later we can split it into:

courses/
  209806.json
  359474.json
  210549.json
Which makes editing safer.

But one master JSON is fine for now.

One Thing I Want To Show You Next
You have 26,000 landers.

But you actually only have about 40 courses.

So there is a much smarter way to generate those pages where:

Course JSON
+
Session JSON
+
Location JSON
+
Occupation JSON
=
Lander page
That architecture will make the whole system dramatically easier to scale.

If you'd like, I can draw the exact data architecture for the 26k lander system next.

Yes. A few do jump out as “must have before we go deeper into the frame.”

The big idea
Before we keep polishing architecture, you want the highest-leverage course set locked so the frame is built around the real business, not around edge cases and junk.

So I would not stop everything and catalog every weird course yet.

I would lock the core demand drivers first.

Must-have next
These are the ones I’d make sure are fully represented before earnest frame work continues:

1. AHA Heartsaver First Aid / CPR / AED In-person ❤️
This is a major money page and a major search page.
You already have the blended version, but the in-person version matters because many customers still want or assume a one-visit class.

2. AHA Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid / CPR / AED Online + In-Person Skills
You already did the in-person pediatric page.
The blended pediatric version is a real offering and likely useful for childcare traffic.

3. HSI Adult First Aid | Adult/Child/Infant CPR AED
This is the cleaner HSI comparison point.
The HSI BLS + Adult First Aid combo is niche and good, but this one is probably the real HSI workplace counterpart to Heartsaver.

4. HSI Adult/Child/Infant CPR AED | Blended Learning
Only if you actually want an HSI blended public-facing option.
If not, skip it and keep HSI mostly corporate/back-pocket.

5. HSI BLS | Blended Learning
This one matters more than some of the HSI oddballs because it is the simplest HSI healthcare-facing alternative.

6. STOP THE BLEED®
This jumps out. Not because it is core revenue, but because it is:

marketable

group-friendly

credibility-building

good for corporate and community lead generation

It deserves a proper page, even if not 4,000 landers.

Important, but only if you want that lane
Bloodborne Pathogens
I don’t see a standalone course type in the list, but I do see the product/add-on.
If you have an actual enrollable course type for it, that is worth capturing.
It is cheap, useful, and good for employer packages.

AHA Heartsaver CPR AED - Online c̄ In-person Skills
You already told me you prefer steering these people to in-person.
So I would not prioritize this unless it is needed as a quiet supporting page.

Not must-have right now
These jump out as not worth slowing the frame down for:

placeholder headers like “Healthcare Provider: BLS Course”

zero-dollar weird variants

corrupted/junk rows

instructor renewals at $0

Spanish variants unless you are truly going after Spanish landers right now

on-site/group versions as if they were normal public course pages

ARC ALS / ARC PALS unless you are actually seeing real demand

PALS Plus modules

ASLS for now

“Personal Event - Not Available”

corrupted nonsense rows

Those should be handled later as:

hidden support items

internal-only catalog items

corporate/group pages

admin cleanup targets

The real shortlist I’d do next
If I were driving the flow, I’d do this next:

AHA Heartsaver First Aid / CPR / AED In-person ❤️

AHA Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid / CPR / AED Online + In-Person Skills

HSI Adult First Aid | Adult/Child/Infant CPR AED

HSI BLS | Blended Learning

STOP THE BLEED®

That set would give us a much more honest “frame complete enough to build around” state.

What this tells me about the final structure
Your eventual public catalog should probably stay something like:

Primary public catalog
AHA BLS

AHA HeartCode BLS

AHA ACLS

AHA HeartCode ACLS

AHA PALS Renewal

AHA HeartCode PALS

AHA Heartsaver CPR AED

AHA Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED

AHA Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid CPR AED

Secondary / support
ARC BLS

HSI workplace options

USCG First Aid

Family & Friends

STOP THE BLEED®

Separate buckets
Instructor courses

Group/on-site courses

headers/placeholders/junk/admin rows

What you should do next
Paste AHA Heartsaver First Aid / CPR / AED In-person ❤️ next.
That feels like the biggest missing piece before we keep tightening the frame.

Course ID
209809

Clean Title
AHA Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED – In-Person

Lander Title
First Aid CPR AED Certification – AHA Heartsaver In-Person Class

URL Slug
/first-aid-cpr-aed-in-person

Schedule URL
https://coastalcprtraining.enrollware.com/schedule#ct209809

Category
Public / Workplace CPR

Certifying Body
AHA

Price
95.00

Card
Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED

Lander HTML
<h2>First Aid CPR AED Certification – AHA Heartsaver In-Person Class</h2>

<p>
The American Heart Association Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED course is an instructor-led classroom program for people who need first aid and CPR certification for work, regulatory compliance, or general preparedness.
</p>

<p>
This class teaches participants how to respond to medical emergencies, injury emergencies, choking, and sudden cardiac arrest using adult, child, and infant CPR and AED skills.
</p>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Workplace safety responders</li>
<li>Teachers and school staff</li>
<li>Coaches and scout leaders</li>
<li>Construction and industrial employees</li>
<li>Non-medical home care workers</li>
<li>Anyone required to have OSHA-aligned first aid and CPR training</li>
</ul>

<h3>What You Will Learn</h3>

<ul>
<li>First aid basics and scene assessment</li>
<li>Medical emergencies</li>
<li>Injury emergencies</li>
<li>Adult, child, and infant CPR</li>
<li>Relief of choking</li>
<li>Use of an Automated External Defibrillator (AED)</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Format</h3>

<p>
This is a traditional instructor-led classroom course with hands-on skills practice and testing.
</p>

<p>
Students complete all training in one visit, making this a practical option for people who want a straightforward in-person certification experience.
</p>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
Students who successfully complete the course receive a
<strong>2-year American Heart Association Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED completion card</strong>.
</p>

<h3>Workplace Compliance</h3>

<p>
This course is commonly used for workplace responders and aligns with OSHA expectations for employees who may need to respond to first aid or cardiac emergencies as part of their job duties.
</p>

<h3>Why This Course Matters</h3>

<p>
For many students, this is the simplest path: one class, one visit, one certification card covering first aid, CPR, and AED.
</p>
SEO Description
Instructor-led AHA Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED training. Hands-on classroom course for workplace responders, teachers, coaches, and non-medical staff. Learn first aid, CPR, choking relief, and AED use with a 2-year certification card.

Why this one matters before deeper frame work
This page gives you the clean split:

Pediatric workplace option

Lander HTML
<h2>Pediatric First Aid CPR AED Certification – AHA Heartsaver (Online + Skills)</h2>

<p>
The American Heart Association Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid CPR AED course prepares caregivers, teachers, and childcare professionals to respond confidently to emergencies involving infants and children.
</p>

<p>
This blended learning format allows students to complete the knowledge portion online at their own pace, followed by a short in-person skills session to demonstrate hands-on CPR and first aid techniques.
</p>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Childcare providers and daycare staff</li>
<li>Teachers and school employees</li>
<li>Summer camp staff</li>
<li>Youth sports coaches</li>
<li>Foster parents and guardians</li>
<li>Babysitters and nannies</li>
<li>Parents and grandparents</li>
</ul>

<h3>What You Will Learn</h3>

<ul>
<li>Pediatric illness recognition and response</li>
<li>Bleeding control and injury care</li>
<li>Allergic reactions and epinephrine use</li>
<li>Asthma emergencies</li>
<li>Bites, stings, and burns</li>
<li>Drowning response</li>
<li>Infant and child CPR</li>
<li>Use of an Automated External Defibrillator (AED)</li>
<li>Relief of choking</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Format</h3>

<p>
This course uses the American Heart Association blended learning format.
</p>

<ul>
<li><strong>Part 1:</strong> Online self-paced training covering pediatric first aid and CPR concepts</li>
<li><strong>Part 2:</strong> In-person skills session with an instructor</li>
</ul>

<p>
The hands-on session focuses on practicing CPR, AED use, and choking relief skills for infants and children.
</p>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
Students who successfully complete both parts receive an 
<strong>American Heart Association Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid CPR AED completion card</strong> valid for two years.
</p>

<h3>Regulatory Use</h3>

<p>
This course is commonly used to meet childcare provider CPR and first aid training requirements in many states, including programs regulated under childcare licensing agencies.
</p>
SEO Description
AHA Heartsaver Pediatric First Aid CPR AED certification for childcare providers, teachers, and caregivers. Blended learning format with online training and hands-on skills session. Certification valid for two years.
One observation (important)
Your Heartsaver catalog is now forming a clean structure:

• Heartsaver First Aid CPR AED (Online + Skills)

Childcare Track
• Pediatric First Aid CPR AED (In-person)
• Pediatric First Aid CPR AED (Online + Skills)

That is exactly how AHA expects training sites to present them, and it will map cleanly into the lander network.

Good. This one belongs in the archive but with a slightly different tone than the AHA pages. The goal is:

acknowledge HSI

<h2>First Aid CPR AED Certification – HSI In-Person Course</h2>

<p>
The HSI Adult First Aid CPR AED course provides practical, instructor-led training for individuals who may need to respond to medical emergencies in the workplace, community, or home.
</p>

<p>
Participants learn how to recognize life-threatening emergencies, provide first aid, perform CPR for adults, children, and infants, and safely use an automated external defibrillator (AED).
</p>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Workplace safety teams</li>
<li>Teachers and school staff</li>
<li>Coaches and athletic staff</li>
<li>Fitness instructors</li>
<li>Residential caregivers</li>
<li>Community members who want practical emergency skills</li>
</ul>

<h3>What You Will Learn</h3>

<ul>
<li>First aid basics and scene safety</li>
<li>Bleeding control and injury response</li>
<li>Burns and shock</li>
<li>Medical emergencies</li>
<li>Adult, child, and infant CPR</li>
<li>Relief of choking</li>
<li>Use of an Automated External Defibrillator (AED)</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Format</h3>

<p>
This is a traditional instructor-led classroom course that includes demonstration, guided practice, and hands-on skills evaluation with a certified instructor.
</p>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
Students who successfully complete the course receive an 
<strong>HSI / ASHI CPR AED and First Aid certification card</strong> valid for two years.
</p>

<h3>Workplace Compliance</h3>

<p>
This training meets OSHA expectations for workplace first aid responders under
<strong>29 CFR 1910.151</strong>.
</p>

<h3>Maritime Credential Option</h3>

<p>
Students pursuing a Merchant Mariner Credential may also receive documentation meeting the
<strong>U.S. Coast Guard Elementary First Aid and CPR requirement</strong>.
</p>

HSI Adult First Aid CPR AED certification class. Instructor-led training covering CPR, AED use, choking relief, and emergency first aid for adults, children, and infants. Meets OSHA workplace training expectations.

<h2>HSI BLS Certification – Online Course with In-Person Skills Session</h2>

<p>
The HSI Basic Life Support (BLS) course prepares participants to recognize life-threatening emergencies and provide high-quality CPR using current resuscitation science and international treatment recommendations.
</p>

<p>
This blended learning format allows students to complete the knowledge portion online followed by an in-person skills session with an instructor.
</p>

<h3>Who This Course Is For</h3>

<ul>
<li>Healthcare workers whose employer accepts HSI BLS certification</li>
<li>Dental offices and outpatient clinics</li>
<li>Emergency response teams</li>
<li>Professional rescuers</li>
<li>Anyone needing professional-level CPR training</li>
</ul>

<h3>What You Will Learn</h3>

<ul>
<li>High-quality CPR for adults, children, and infants</li>
<li>Use of an Automated External Defibrillator (AED)</li>
<li>Team-based resuscitation principles</li>
<li>Relief of choking</li>
<li>Scene safety and patient assessment</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Format</h3>

<p>
This course uses a blended learning format:
</p>

<ul>
<li><strong>Part 1:</strong> Online self-paced training modules</li>
<li><strong>Part 2:</strong> In-person hands-on skills evaluation with an instructor</li>
</ul>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
Students who successfully complete both parts receive an 
<strong>HSI Basic Life Support certification card</strong> valid for two years.
</p>

<h3>Choosing the Right BLS Course</h3>

<p>
Many hospitals and healthcare employers require the American Heart Association (AHA) BLS certification. Students should confirm employer requirements before enrolling in an HSI BLS course.
</p>

<div style="text-indent: 0px; text-align: justify;"><strong><img src="https://www.enrollware.com/sitefiles/coastalcprtraining/STB_I.png" alt="" width="200" align="right" /></strong>
<h3>STOP THE <span style="color: #ff0000;">BLEED</span><sup>&reg;</sup>&nbsp;Interactive Course</h3>
<p><em>Stop The Bleed</em> is a national campaign that aims to make bleeding control training as commonplace as CPR training. Since a person can bleed out and die in as little as three minutes it is literally a matter of life or death that someone take action immediately, someone who is already there. Remember, until help arrives, YOU are the help.</p>
<p>This interactive course is a virtual alternative to the in-person lecture presentation. The <em>STOP THE BLEED<sup>&reg;</sup></em>&nbsp;Interactive Course guides individuals through the three methods of bleeding control using video demonstrations, interactive learning, and spontaneous quizzes.</p>
<p><em><strong>910CPR</strong> </em>believes strongly that this information is valuable and want to bring this free program to your attention.&nbsp;<strong> The online&nbsp;<em>STOP THE BLEED<sup>&reg;</sup></em>&nbsp;Interactive Course is free to all</strong> and can be taken multiple times to learn and refresh knowledge of the material.&nbsp;</p>
<p style="text-align: justify;">Although the information above is free, to&nbsp;recieve a&nbsp;<em>STOP THE BLEED<sup>&reg;</sup></em> certification, there is a nominal fee to cover materials and manikin wear.&nbsp; There is no obligation to obtain the certification, but it is&nbsp;encouraged that you to choose the practial training to be confident in your understanding of the material.</p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
</div>

This is a very good one to include, and you’re already positioning it correctly.

The key with STOP THE BLEED® is that it is not really a revenue course. It is a credibility course and a lead generator.

It tells corporate safety managers, schools, churches, and public agencies:

These guys are serious about emergency preparedness, not just selling CPR cards.

So the lander version should lean into public safety + workplace preparedness, not just the free online training.

Lander HTML
<h2>STOP THE BLEED® Training – Learn to Control Life-Threatening Bleeding</h2>

<p>
Severe bleeding is the <strong>number one preventable cause of death after traumatic injury</strong>. 
The STOP THE BLEED® program teaches ordinary people how to recognize life-threatening bleeding and take immediate action until professional help arrives.
</p>

<p>
In many emergencies, the person who saves a life is not a doctor or paramedic. It is someone already on scene. 
<strong>Until help arrives, you are the help.</strong>
</p>

<h3>What You Will Learn</h3>

<ul>
<li>How to recognize life-threatening bleeding</li>
<li>How to apply direct pressure</li>
<li>How to pack a wound</li>
<li>How to properly apply a tourniquet</li>
<li>How to respond safely in traumatic emergencies</li>
</ul>

<h3>Course Options</h3>

<p>
Participants may complete the <strong>STOP THE BLEED® interactive online training</strong> for free.
</p>

<p>
For those who want hands-on experience, a short in-person skills session is available to practice bleeding control techniques using training equipment.
</p>

<h3>Who Should Take This Training</h3>

<ul>
<li>Teachers and school staff</li>
<li>Construction and industrial workers</li>
<li>Security personnel</li>
<li>Church safety teams</li>
<li>Coaches and athletic staff</li>
<li>Parents and community members</li>
</ul>

<h3>Why This Training Matters</h3>

<p>
A person with severe bleeding can die in <strong>three to five minutes</strong>. Emergency responders may not arrive in time. 
Training everyday people to control bleeding can save lives in car crashes, workplace accidents, natural disasters, or acts of violence.
</p>

<h3>Certification</h3>

<p>
The online STOP THE BLEED® training is available at no cost.  
Participants who complete a hands-on training session may receive documentation confirming practical bleeding control training.
</p>

On the image question: not yet, not fully. I have been preserving the HTML snippets and their image references, but I have not yet done a full scan-and-download pass to mirror those images into your GitHub repo. And for this project, I think we should.

What should happen with images
For anything we plan to keep using in lander HTML, the safer long-term move is:

extract every image URL from the archived HTML

download the ones we control or want to preserve

place them in your repo, likely under something like:

/docs/images/course/

or /docs/images/enrollware-mirror/

then eventually swap HTML image references from Enrollware-hosted URLs to your repo-hosted URLs where appropriate

That matters because:

Enrollware image paths can change

some image names are ugly or temporary

GitHub-hosted assets are easier to manage for a 26k-page system

you want the lander project to be self-sufficient, not dependent on Enrollware staying unchanged

My recommendation
Do not blindly download every image yet.

Instead do it in two passes:

Pass 1: must-keep assets
Mirror these first:

logos

hero/course cover images

diagrams that appear in multiple landers

specialty images like STOP THE BLEED, USCG, pediatric course visuals

Pass 2: cleanup / replacement
Then decide which Enrollware images should be:

kept as-is

renamed for SEO

replaced with better branded assets

dropped entirely

I scan course_archive_v2.json for every image URL and generate an image manifest.

That manifest should tell us:

which images are referenced

how many times

which course IDs use them

proposed local filenames

That gives you a controlled download plan instead of a mess.

Thought for 18s
Mirror Course Archive Images
· powershell
param(
        $attempts++
        try {
            Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $Url -OutFile $DestinationPath -UseBasicParsing
            return "downloaded"
        }
        catch {
            if ($attempts -ge $maxAttempts) {
                throw
            }
            Start-Sleep -Seconds 1
        }
    }
}

$resolvedArchive = Resolve-Path -LiteralPath $ArchiveJsonPath
$resolvedRepoRoot = Resolve-Path -LiteralPath $RepoRoot

$imagesFolderFull = Join-Path $resolvedRepoRoot $ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo
$updatedArchiveFull = Join-Path $resolvedRepoRoot $UpdatedArchiveRelativeToRepo
$manifestFull = Join-Path $resolvedRepoRoot $ManifestRelativeToRepo

Ensure-Folder -Path $imagesFolderFull
Ensure-Folder -Path ([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName($updatedArchiveFull))
Ensure-Folder -Path ([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName($manifestFull))

Write-Info "Loading archive: $resolvedArchive"
$archive = Get-Content -LiteralPath $resolvedArchive -Raw | ConvertFrom-Json -Depth 100

if (-not $archive.courses) {
    throw "Archive does not contain a 'courses' array."
}

$imageRegistry = @{}
$manifestItems = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[object]

foreach ($course in $archive.courses) {
    $courseId = [string]$course.course_id
    Write-Info "Scanning course $courseId"

    foreach ($fieldName in @('original_html', 'lander_html')) {
        $html = $course.$fieldName
        if ([string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($html)) {
            continue
        }

        $matches = Get-ImageMatches -Html $html
        if ($matches.Count -eq 0) {
            continue
        }

        $updatedHtml = $html

        foreach ($match in $matches) {
            $url = $match.Url
            $hash = Get-HashString -Value $url

            if (-not $imageRegistry.ContainsKey($url)) {
                $ext = Get-ExtensionFromUrl -Url $url
                $baseName = "course-$courseId-$fieldName-$hash$ext"
                $safeName = Get-SafeFileName -Name $baseName
                $destPath = Join-Path $imagesFolderFull $safeName
                $relativePath = ($RelativeImagePathPrefix.TrimEnd('/')) + "/" + $safeName

                try {
                    $status = Download-File -Url $url -DestinationPath $destPath -SkipExisting:$SkipExisting -WhatIf:$WhatIf
                    $imageRegistry[$url] = [pscustomobject]@{
                        url           = $url
                        local_filename = $safeName
                        repo_path      = ($ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo.TrimEnd('/')) + "/" + $safeName
                        relative_src   = $relativePath
                        status         = $status
                    }
                }
                catch {
                    Write-WarnMsg "Failed to download $url : $($_.Exception.Message)"
                    $imageRegistry[$url] = [pscustomobject]@{
                        url           = $url
                        local_filename = $null
                        repo_path      = $null
                        relative_src   = $url
                        status         = "failed"
                    }
                }
            }

            $mapped = $imageRegistry[$url]
            $replacement = 'src="' + $mapped.relative_src + '"'
            $escapedUrl = [System.Text.RegularExpressions.Regex]::Escape($url)
            $updatedHtml = [System.Text.RegularExpressions.Regex]::Replace(
                $updatedHtml,
                'src\s*=\s*["'']' + $escapedUrl + '["'']',
                $replacement,
                [System.Text.RegularExpressions.RegexOptions]::IgnoreCase
            )

            $manifestItems.Add([pscustomobject]@{
                course_id     = $courseId
                field         = $fieldName
                original_url  = $url
                local_filename = $mapped.local_filename
                repo_path     = $mapped.repo_path
                relative_src  = $mapped.relative_src
                status        = $mapped.status
            })
        }

        $course.$fieldName = $updatedHtml
    }
}

$archive.version = if ($archive.version) { [int]$archive.version + 1 } else { 1 }
$archive.image_mirror = [pscustomobject]@{
    generated_utc = (Get-Date).ToUniversalTime().ToString("yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ")
    images_folder = $ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo
    relative_src_prefix = $RelativeImagePathPrefix
    image_count = @($imageRegistry.Keys).Count
}

$archive | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 100 | Set-Content -LiteralPath $updatedArchiveFull -Encoding UTF8

$manifest = [pscustomobject]@{
    generated_utc = (Get-Date).ToUniversalTime().ToString("yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ")
    source_archive = [string]$resolvedArchive
    updated_archive = $UpdatedArchiveRelativeToRepo
    images_folder = $ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo
    image_count = @($imageRegistry.Keys).Count
    items = $manifestItems
}

$manifest | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 100 | Set-Content -LiteralPath $manifestFull -Encoding UTF8

Write-Info "Updated archive written to: $updatedArchiveFull"
Write-Info "Manifest written to: $manifestFull"
Write-Info ("Unique images found: " + @($imageRegistry.Keys).Count)

Write-Host "`nExample usage:" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host 'pwsh ./mirror_course_archive_images.ps1 -ArchiveJsonPath ".\docs\data\course_archive_v2.json" -RepoRoot "."' -ForegroundColor White

Yes. That is the right next move.

I wrote the PowerShell script for exactly that workflow:

scan the archive JSON for image src= references

download each image into a repo data/images folder

rewrite the HTML in the archive to use repo-relative image paths

write an updated archive plus an image manifest

Script
Use this as mirror_course_archive_images.ps1:

param(
    [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
    [string]$ArchiveJsonPath,

    [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
    [string]$RepoRoot,

    [string]$ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo = "docs/images/course-archive",

    [string]$UpdatedArchiveRelativeToRepo = "docs/data/course_archive_mirrored.json",

    [string]$ManifestRelativeToRepo = "docs/data/course_image_manifest.json",

    [string]$RelativeImagePathPrefix = "../images/course-archive",

    [switch]$SkipExisting,

    [switch]$WhatIf
)

Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
$ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"

function Write-Info {
    param([string]$Message)
    Write-Host "[INFO] $Message" -ForegroundColor Cyan
}

function Write-WarnMsg {
    param([string]$Message)
    Write-Host "[WARN] $Message" -ForegroundColor Yellow
}

function Get-SafeFileName {
    param([string]$Name)

    $safe = $Name.ToLowerInvariant()
    $safe = $safe -replace '%20', '-'
    $safe = $safe -replace '[^a-z0-9._-]', '-'
    $safe = $safe -replace '-{2,}', '-'
    $safe = $safe.Trim('-')

    if ([string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($safe)) {
        return "file"
    }

    return $safe
}

function Get-ExtensionFromUrl {
    param([string]$Url)

    try {
        $uri = [System.Uri]$Url
        $leaf = [System.IO.Path]::GetFileName($uri.AbsolutePath)
        $ext = [System.IO.Path]::GetExtension($leaf)
        if (-not [string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($ext)) {
            return $ext.ToLowerInvariant()
        }
    }
    catch {
    }

    return ".bin"
}

function Get-HashString {
    param([string]$Value)

    $sha = [System.Security.Cryptography.SHA256]::Create()
    try {
        $bytes = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes($Value)
        $hashBytes = $sha.ComputeHash($bytes)
        $hash = [System.BitConverter]::ToString($hashBytes).Replace("-", "").ToLowerInvariant()
        return $hash.Substring(0, 12)
    }
    finally {
        $sha.Dispose()
    }
}

function Get-ImageMatches {
    param([string]$Html)

    if ([string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($Html)) {
        return @()
    }

    $pattern = 'src\s*=\s*["''](?<url>[^"'']+)["'']'
    $matches = [System.Text.RegularExpressions.Regex]::Matches(
        $Html,
        $pattern,
        [System.Text.RegularExpressions.RegexOptions]::IgnoreCase
    )

    $results = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[object]
    foreach ($match in $matches) {
        $url = $match.Groups['url'].Value
        if ($url -match '^https?://') {
            $results.Add([pscustomobject]@{
                Original = $match.Value
                Url      = $url
            })
        }
    }

    return $results
}

function Ensure-Folder {
    param([string]$Path)

    if (-not (Test-Path -LiteralPath $Path)) {
        New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $Path -Force | Out-Null
    }
}

function Download-File {
    param(
        [string]$Url,
        [string]$DestinationPath,
        [switch]$SkipExisting,
        [switch]$WhatIf
    )

    if ($SkipExisting -and (Test-Path -LiteralPath $DestinationPath)) {
        return "skipped-existing"
    }

    if ($WhatIf) {
        return "whatif"
    }

    $attempts = 0
    $maxAttempts = 3
    while ($true) {
        $attempts++
        try {
            Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $Url -OutFile $DestinationPath -UseBasicParsing
            return "downloaded"
        }
        catch {
            if ($attempts -ge $maxAttempts) {
                throw
            }
            Start-Sleep -Seconds 1
        }
    }
}

$resolvedArchive = Resolve-Path -LiteralPath $ArchiveJsonPath
$resolvedRepoRoot = Resolve-Path -LiteralPath $RepoRoot

$imagesFolderFull = Join-Path $resolvedRepoRoot $ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo
$updatedArchiveFull = Join-Path $resolvedRepoRoot $UpdatedArchiveRelativeToRepo
$manifestFull = Join-Path $resolvedRepoRoot $ManifestRelativeToRepo

Ensure-Folder -Path $imagesFolderFull
Ensure-Folder -Path ([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName($updatedArchiveFull))
Ensure-Folder -Path ([System.IO.Path]::GetDirectoryName($manifestFull))

Write-Info "Loading archive: $resolvedArchive"
$archive = Get-Content -LiteralPath $resolvedArchive -Raw | ConvertFrom-Json -Depth 100

if (-not $archive.courses) {
    throw "Archive does not contain a 'courses' array."
}

$imageRegistry = @{}
$manifestItems = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[object]

foreach ($course in $archive.courses) {
    $courseId = [string]$course.course_id
    Write-Info "Scanning course $courseId"

    foreach ($fieldName in @('original_html', 'lander_html')) {
        $html = $course.$fieldName
        if ([string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($html)) {
            continue
        }

        $matches = Get-ImageMatches -Html $html
        if ($matches.Count -eq 0) {
            continue
        }

        $updatedHtml = $html

        foreach ($match in $matches) {
            $url = $match.Url
            $hash = Get-HashString -Value $url

            if (-not $imageRegistry.ContainsKey($url)) {
                $ext = Get-ExtensionFromUrl -Url $url
                $baseName = "course-$courseId-$fieldName-$hash$ext"
                $safeName = Get-SafeFileName -Name $baseName
                $destPath = Join-Path $imagesFolderFull $safeName
                $relativePath = ($RelativeImagePathPrefix.TrimEnd('/')) + "/" + $safeName

                try {
                    $status = Download-File -Url $url -DestinationPath $destPath -SkipExisting:$SkipExisting -WhatIf:$WhatIf
                    $imageRegistry[$url] = [pscustomobject]@{
                        url            = $url
                        local_filename = $safeName
                        repo_path      = ($ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo.TrimEnd('/')) + "/" + $safeName
                        relative_src   = $relativePath
                        status         = $status
                    }
                }
                catch {
                    Write-WarnMsg "Failed to download $url : $($_.Exception.Message)"
                    $imageRegistry[$url] = [pscustomobject]@{
                        url            = $url
                        local_filename = $null
                        repo_path      = $null
                        relative_src   = $url
                        status         = "failed"
                    }
                }
            }

            $mapped = $imageRegistry[$url]
            $replacement = 'src="' + $mapped.relative_src + '"'
            $escapedUrl = [System.Text.RegularExpressions.Regex]::Escape($url)

            $updatedHtml = [System.Text.RegularExpressions.Regex]::Replace(
                $updatedHtml,
                'src\s*=\s*["'']' + $escapedUrl + '["'']',
                $replacement,
                [System.Text.RegularExpressions.RegexOptions]::IgnoreCase
            )

            $manifestItems.Add([pscustomobject]@{
                course_id      = $courseId
                field          = $fieldName
                original_url   = $url
                local_filename = $mapped.local_filename
                repo_path      = $mapped.repo_path
                relative_src   = $mapped.relative_src
                status         = $mapped.status
            })
        }

        $course.$fieldName = $updatedHtml
    }
}

$archive.version = if ($archive.version) { [int]$archive.version + 1 } else { 1 }
$archive.image_mirror = [pscustomobject]@{
    generated_utc       = (Get-Date).ToUniversalTime().ToString("yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ")
    images_folder       = $ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo
    relative_src_prefix = $RelativeImagePathPrefix
    image_count         = @($imageRegistry.Keys).Count
}

$archive | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 100 | Set-Content -LiteralPath $updatedArchiveFull -Encoding UTF8

$manifest = [pscustomobject]@{
    generated_utc  = (Get-Date).ToUniversalTime().ToString("yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ")
    source_archive = [string]$resolvedArchive
    updated_archive = $UpdatedArchiveRelativeToRepo
    images_folder  = $ImagesFolderRelativeToRepo
    image_count    = @($imageRegistry.Keys).Count
    items          = $manifestItems
}

$manifest | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 100 | Set-Content -LiteralPath $manifestFull -Encoding UTF8

Write-Info "Updated archive written to: $updatedArchiveFull"
Write-Info "Manifest written to: $manifestFull"
Write-Info ("Unique images found: " + @($imageRegistry.Keys).Count)

Write-Host "`nExample usage:" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host 'pwsh ./mirror_course_archive_images.ps1 -ArchiveJsonPath ".\docs\data\course_archive_v2.json" -RepoRoot "."' -ForegroundColor White
How I would use it
Put the script in your repo, probably here:

/scripts/mirror_course_archive_images.ps1
Then run it from repo root like this:

pwsh ./scripts/mirror_course_archive_images.ps1 `
  -ArchiveJsonPath ".\docs\data\course_archive_v2.json" `
  -RepoRoot "."
It will produce:

mirrored images in:

/docs/images/course-archive/
updated archive:

/docs/data/course_archive_mirrored.json
image manifest:

/docs/data/course_image_manifest.json
Important implementation note
Right now this script updates image paths in the JSON HTML fields. That is the right first step.

Later, when your page generator builds actual HTML files, those pages should either:

use the already-updated HTML from course_archive_mirrored.json, or

use the manifest to rewrite paths again if needed.

One thing to watch
For HSI BLS | Blended Learning I had no source HTML snippet, so its original_html is blank in v2. That is fine. The script just skips blanks.

Strong recommendation
Before you run this on the real repo, test it once with:

-WhatIf
Then run again with:

-SkipExisting
That will keep re-runs cleaner.

If you want, the next best move is for me to write the paired script that converts course_archive_mirrored.json into one JSON file per course for the generator.


Supply values for the following parameters:
ArchiveJsonPath: D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers
RepoRoot:??


You accidentally pointed the script at the folder, not the JSON file.

The script expects:

ArchiveJsonPath → the actual JSON file

RepoRoot → the root of your GitHub repo

Given your repo structure, it should look like this.

Correct values
ArchiveJsonPath

D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers\docs\data\course_archive_v2.json
RepoRoot

D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers
Example command (clean version)
Run this from anywhere:

pwsh .\scripts\mirror_course_archive_images.ps1 `
-ArchiveJsonPath "D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers\docs\data\course_archive_v2.json" `
-RepoRoot "D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers"
What will happen
The script will:

Scan the HTML inside course_archive_v2.json

Find every <img src="">

Download the image

Save it to:

/docs/images/course-archive/
Example result:

docs/
   images/
      course-archive/
         course-344486-original_html-82d1a3e.png
Update the HTML inside the JSON to point to:

../images/course-archive/filename.jpg
Write a new archive:

docs/data/course_archive_mirrored.json
Generate an image inventory:

docs/data/course_image_manifest.json
Pro move (first run)
Run it once in dry-run mode:

pwsh .\scripts\mirror_course_archive_images.ps1 `
-ArchiveJsonPath "D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers\docs\data\course_archive_v2.json" `
-RepoRoot "D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers" `
-WhatIf
Then run it for real.

One more thing (important for the 26k landers)
After this runs, the project will have:

COURSE DATA
docs/data/course_archive_mirrored.json

COURSE IMAGES
docs/images/course-archive/

COURSE IMAGE MAP
docs/data/course_image_manifest.json
That means your entire course catalog becomes portable.
Even if Enrollware changes everything later.

Exactly what you wanted when you said:

YOU WERE THE REPOSITORY

Now the repo actually becomes the repository.

docs/courses/
   209806.json
   344486.json
   448630.json
That makes the 26,000-page lander generator MUCH faster.


Supply values for the following parameters:
ArchiveJsonPath: d:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers\raw\course_archive_v1.json
RepoRoot: d:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers\
[INFO] Loading archive: D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers\raw\course_archive_v1.json
[INFO] Scanning course 209806
: The property 'Count' cannot be found on this object. Verify that the property exists.
PS D:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers>

Good. You ran it correctly. 👍
The error tells us exactly where the script broke.

What happened
PowerShell sometimes returns a single object instead of an array when there is only one match.

So this line fails when there is only one <img> tag:

if ($matches.Count -eq 0)
Because $matches becomes a single object, not a collection.

Classic PowerShell quirk.

Fix (simple and safe)
Find this line in the script:

$matches = Get-ImageMatches -Html $html
Replace it with:

$matches = @(Get-ImageMatches -Html $html)
The @() forces PowerShell to always treat it as an array, even if there's only one result.

Then change this block
Replace:

if ($matches.Count -eq 0) {
    continue
}
with:

if ($matches.Count -eq 0) {
    continue
}
(no change needed once @() is used)

Why this fix works
@() forces PowerShell to treat results as:

0 items  -> empty array
1 item   -> array with 1 element
many     -> array
Without it PowerShell does this:

0 items  -> $null
1 item   -> object
many     -> array
Which breaks .Count.

After the fix
Run again:

pwsh ./scripts/mirror_course_archive_images.ps1 `
-ArchiveJsonPath "d:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers\raw\course_archive_v1.json" `
-RepoRoot "d:\Users\ten77\Documents\GitHub\910cpr-class-landers"
You should start seeing:

[INFO] Scanning course 209806
[INFO] Scanning course 344486
[INFO] Scanning course 448630
Then images begin downloading.

Small improvement I recommend next
Once this runs successfully, we should add one more protection:

User-Agent header
because some sites block Invoke-WebRequest.

Takes 3 lines to harden the downloader.

If you'd like, I can also give you the fully corrected v2 of the script so you don't have to patch anything manually. It will also:

• download faster
• avoid duplicate images
• retry failures
• produce cleaner filenames for SEO.

And it will make the 26,000 lander build smoother.

Yes. That explains it.

Your current image script is almost certainly walking:
